planting.note: Sow after frost danger eases; a single spring planting can crop until fall.

directions: Sow seed clusters half an inch deep and thin to one strong plant per station. Keep plants evenly watered for lush growth of crimson-stalked, dark green leaves. Harvest outer leaves regularly and the plant will keep producing through the season.

planting.note: A leaf beet that resists bolting; sow spring and late summer for year-round leaves.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin to give each clump room to bush out. Keep well watered so the tender spinach-like leaves stay mild and quick to regrow. Pick the outer leaves often to keep new growth coming through heat and light frost.

planting.note: Grown for its striped stems; sow after the last frost for a long harvest.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin to one plant per spot for full-sized leaves. Water steadily so the pink-and-white candy-striped stalks stay crisp and colorful. Harvest outer leaves young for salads or larger for cooking, and the plant keeps yielding.

planting.note: A hardy Italian heirloom; sow spring through late summer for a durable crop.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin to strong single plants for best size. Keep evenly watered to grow broad, savoyed leaves on wide silvery-white stems. Harvest outer leaves as needed; the sturdy plants tolerate heat and light frost alike.

planting.note: A winter radish; sow midsummer to late summer for a fall storage crop.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin generously so the large roots can develop. Water steadily through the cooling season for firm, black-skinned globes. Harvest before hard freeze and store cool; the pungent white flesh keeps for months.

planting.note: This long daikon needs deep loose soil; sow in late summer for fall roots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in deeply worked soil so the long white roots grow straight. Thin plants well apart and keep evenly watered for smooth, mild roots. Harvest before hard frost when roots reach a foot or more, lifting carefully to avoid breakage.

planting.note: A classic pickling daikon; sow late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep in deep, loose soil and thin the seedlings well apart. Water steadily to grow the crisp cylindrical white roots with green shoulders. Harvest in fall for fresh use or slice and salt for traditional pickles.

planting.note: Sow in cool spring and fall, repeating every two weeks for a steady crop.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin to about an inch apart. Keep soil evenly moist for fast growth of the round red roots with white tips. Harvest promptly at globe size, before the crisp mild flesh turns spongy.

planting.note: An unusual yellow Polish radish; sow in the cool of spring and fall.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings to an inch apart. Keep soil moist to grow round roots with distinctive dusty-yellow skin. Harvest at globe size for firm white flesh with a pleasant nutty bite.

planting.note: A salad turnip eaten raw; sow in cool spring and fall for the sweetest roots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings to a couple inches apart. Keep evenly watered for fast growth of smooth, pure-white salad roots. Harvest small at one to two inches when the flesh is sweet, crisp, and mild enough to eat fresh.

planting.note: A red-skinned salad turnip; sow spring and late summer for tender roots.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings for well-rounded roots. Water steadily to grow flat-round roots with bright red skin and white flesh. Harvest young for crisp, mild roots good raw in salads or lightly cooked.

planting.note: Grown for greens rather than roots; sow spring and fall for tender leaves.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings to a few inches apart. Keep soil moist for a lush stand of dark green leaves, since the roots stay tough and small. Cut the tender tops repeatedly for cooked greens, and expect them to sweeten after frost.

planting.note: Sow in mid to late summer so the large roots mature in the cool of fall.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings well apart so the big roots can size up. Keep soil evenly moist through the long season for firm, smooth roots with purple crowns and yellow flesh. Harvest after light frosts, which sweeten the flavor, and store cool for winter.

planting.note: A classic storage swede; sow in summer for a fall and winter harvest.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin plants generously to grow uniform globes. Water steadily so the roots develop smooth purple-topped shoulders and sweet yellow flesh. Harvest after frost and store cool, where these roots keep dependably for months.

planting.note: A Vermont heirloom; sow in mid to late summer for fall roots sweetened by frost.

directions: Sow seed half an inch deep and thin seedlings to give roots room to swell. Keep soil moist for creamy white roots with fine-grained, mild flesh. Harvest after frost, which brings out the sweetness, and store cool for winter cooking.

planting.note: Sow a few weeks before the last frost and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Bred in Israel for heat, this upright romaine bolts far later than most and holds sweet in warm spells. Sow seed about a quarter inch deep and thin plants to a hand's width apart. Cut the whole head at the base once the tall leaves feel dense and crisp.

planting.note: Plant in early spring and again in late summer, avoiding the peak of summer heat.

directions: This tall, glossy green cos forms dense uniform heads that stand well in the field. Barely cover the tiny seed and keep the surface damp until sprouts appear. Firm the plants by spacing them evenly, and harvest whole heads before they begin to stretch upward.

planting.note: Sow in cool weather; this variety carries useful resistance to downy mildew.

directions: A dependable dark-green cos with broad, slightly savoyed leaves and a crunchy heart. Sow shallowly in loose soil and keep moisture even to prevent tip burn. Harvest the full head once the center leaves blanch pale and firm up.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. longifolia 'Craquerelle du Midi'

planting.note: This cold-tolerant French cos suits early spring and overwintering in mild regions.

directions: An old southern-French romaine prized for cold tolerance and a notably crisp, nutty texture. Direct sow thinly and thin to give each rosette room to firm up. In cool weather it develops its best crunch, so cut heads before hot days arrive.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. longifolia 'Lobjoits Green Cos'

planting.note: A hardy British cos that can be sown for spring, summer, or overwintered under cover.

directions: A large, deep-green English cos with thick blistered leaves and a self-folding heart. Sow shallowly and thin generously since heads grow tall and heavy. Harvest when the interior has blanched and the leaves snap cleanly.

planting.note: A large heirloom cos that appreciates rich soil and steady moisture to size up.

directions: This oversized French cos swells into a plump, pale-hearted head named for its balloon-like fullness. Sow seed just below the surface and give plants wide spacing to reach full size. Feed lightly and water consistently, then cut whole heads at the base when firm.

planting.note: A vigorous romaine with strong mildew resistance for spring and fall sowings.

directions: A robust green cos with heavy, well-filled hearts and dependable disease resistance. Sow shallowly and thin to a full hand's width for large heads. Keep moisture even and harvest before warm weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: An Italian market favorite sown in succession from spring through early fall.

directions: This classic Italian loosehead forms a soft, blonde-green rosette with a mild, buttery flavor. Sow thinly and thin the seedlings to salad spacing, using the thinnings young. Pick outer leaves as needed or cut the whole loose head when it fills out.

planting.note: Sow in succession; leaves color up red-bronze in cool weather and bright sun.

directions: Named for its broad, cupped leaves flushed red at the tips over a green base. Scatter seed shallowly and thin to give each plant room to spread. Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again picking, or cut the whole loose rosette young.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. capitata 'Reine des Glaces'

planting.note: This French crisphead heads best in the cool of spring or fall.

directions: The Ice Queen forms a dense green head fringed with sharply cut, jagged leaf edges. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing so the head can firm up. Harvest whole when the interior feels solid and the outer leaves stay crisp.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant summer crisp that resists bolting and tip burn in warm spells.

directions: A glossy green Batavian type that stays sweet and crunchy even as heat builds. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing to form a loose, thick-leaved head. Cut whole heads at the base or strip outer leaves as they enlarge.

planting.note: One of the most heat-tolerant lettuces, holding well through midsummer sowings.

directions: An exceptionally slow-bolting green Batavian with tightly ruffled, pale-green leaves. Sow shallowly and keep soil moist through germination during warm weather. Harvest as a dense loose head or pick leaves for repeated cuttings.

planting.note: A red-tinged summer crisp with good heat tolerance for spring through fall.

directions: A shiny Batavian with green leaves brushed rose-red and a crisp, juicy bite. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing so heads develop weight. Harvest whole once the head feels dense, or take outer leaves as they size up.

planting.note: A deep-red French Batavian that colors best with full sun and cool nights.

directions: This vivid red Batavian holds glossy, crunchy leaves and resists bolting into warm weather. Sow shallowly and thin plants to give the head room to fill. Cut whole heads at the base when firm, or harvest outer leaves for salads.

planting.note: A large, slow-bolting green summer crisp suited to warm-season plantings.

directions: A big, upright green Batavian with thick, wavy leaves and a sweet, crunchy heart. Sow shallowly and thin generously for full-sized heads. Harvest the whole head before hot weather, or pick leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop.

planting.note: A savoyed green Batavian valued for heat tolerance and long field holding.

directions: A heavily savoyed green summer crisp with a crunchy texture and strong bolt resistance. Sow shallowly and keep soil evenly moist until seedlings establish. Cut whole heads at the base when dense, or strip outer leaves as needed.

planting.note: A red summer crisp that holds color and stays sweet through hot weather.

directions: A deep-red Batavian prized for summer heat tolerance and lasting crunch. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing for a well-filled head. Harvest the whole head when dense or take outer leaves for repeated salad cuttings.

planting.note: A Hawaiian-bred crisphead that resists bolting and bitterness in the heat.

directions: Developed in Hawaii for tropical heat, this green crisphead stays mild when others turn bitter. Sow shallowly and keep soil moist through germination in warm soil. Harvest the loose, crunchy head before it sends up a flower stalk.

planting.note: A firm iceberg-type for spring and fall; needs cool weather to head well.

directions: A solid, well-wrapped iceberg producing dense pale heads with crisp ribs. Sow shallowly and thin to wide spacing so heads can fully develop. Harvest when the head feels firm and heavy under gentle pressure.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. crispa 'Red Deer Tongue'

planting.note: Sow in succession; leaves flush deepest red in cool weather and bright sun.

directions: A red-flushed heirloom with triangular, pointed leaves forming a compact rosette. Scatter seed shallowly and thin to salad spacing. Harvest outer leaves for repeated pickings or cut the tidy rosette whole.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. crispa 'Amish Deer Tongue'

planting.note: A hardy heirloom that tolerates heat better than most and stands well.

directions: An old green Amish heirloom with sharply pointed, upright leaves and good bolt resistance. Sow shallowly and thin to give each rosette room. Pick outer leaves as needed or cut the whole plant while young and tender.

common_name: Really Red Deer Tongue Leaf Lettuce
scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. crispa 'Really Red Deer Tongue'

planting.note: Selected for intense red color; sow in succession for a steady supply.

directions: A darker selection of the deer tongue type with deeply red-saturated pointed leaves. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing for full color development. Harvest outer leaves repeatedly or cut the compact rosette whole.

planting.note: A slow-bolting heirloom that colors bronze-red in sun and cool nights.

directions: An heirloom loose-leaf with arrow-shaped leaves washed bronze over green. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing, using thinnings in the kitchen. Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again picking before the plant bolts.

planting.note: One of the darkest red lettuces; color deepens with cool nights and sun.

directions: Prized as one of the deepest wine-red loose-leaf lettuces, with crumpled glossy leaves. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing for best coloring. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the loose rosette whole.

planting.note: A vigorous, heat-tolerant green leaf that stands well before bolting.

directions: A large, ruffled bright-green loose-leaf with thick, crunchy leaves and long standing ability. Sow shallowly and thin to give the wide rosette room. Pick outer leaves repeatedly or cut whole while the leaves stay tender.

planting.note: A productive early green leaf with good bolt and tip-burn resistance.

directions: A dark-green, heavily frilled loose-leaf that grows fast and yields heavily. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing. Harvest outer leaves for repeated cuttings or take the whole loose head while tender.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. crispa 'Royal Oak Leaf'

planting.note: A heat-tolerant oakleaf that resists bolting better than older strains.

directions: An improved green oakleaf with lobed leaves forming a full, dense rosette. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing. Harvest outer leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop or cut the whole rosette when full.

planting.note: A large French red oakleaf that colors bronze-red in cool sunny weather.

directions: A big French oakleaf with elongated, bronze-red lobed leaves and a nutty flavor. Sow shallowly and thin to give the large rosette room to spread. Harvest outer leaves repeatedly or cut the whole plant when it fills out.

planting.note: A frilly green oakleaf with strong bolt resistance for spring and fall.

directions: A vigorous, bright-green oakleaf with densely frilled, deeply lobed leaves. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing for a large rosette. Pick outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head before warm weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: A deeply cut red oakleaf that holds color and stands well in the garden.

directions: A striking red oakleaf with finely serrated, deep-red lobed leaves in a full rosette. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing for best color. Harvest outer leaves for repeated cuttings or cut the whole rosette when full.

planting.note: A cold-hardy French leaf good for overwintering and early spring crops.

directions: A hardy French loose-leaf with crisp, red-tinged blistered leaves that shrugs off cold. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing. Harvest outer leaves through cool weather or cut the whole plant when it fills out.

planting.note: A slow-bolting bronze-tinted leaf that tolerates heat reasonably well.

directions: A tender loose-leaf with rounded green leaves washed bronze at the edges. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing, eating the thinnings. Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again picking before the plant bolts.

scientific_name: Lactuca sativa var. crispa 'Flashy Butter Oak'

planting.note: A buttery oakleaf speckled with red; sow in succession spring and fall.

directions: A tender green oakleaf softly speckled with maroon and holding a buttery texture. Sow shallowly and thin to salad spacing. Harvest outer leaves for repeated pickings or cut the whole rosette when it fills.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant heirloom butterhead that stands well before bolting.

directions: An heirloom butterhead forming a loose green head with bronze-tinged outer leaves. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing so the head can loosely fold. Harvest the whole head when the soft center leaves feel buttery.

planting.note: An heirloom butterhead with red-blushed outer leaves over a green heart.

directions: A large heirloom butterhead whose broad outer leaves flush deep red over a pale-green center. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest the whole head once the soft heart feels tender and full.

planting.note: A German heirloom butterhead speckled red-brown and tolerant of heat.

directions: A German butterhead, also called Sprenkel, with green leaves mottled red-brown and a sweet heart. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest the whole head when the buttery center firms up but before hot weather.

planting.note: A large red butterhead with strong disease resistance for spring and fall.

directions: A big, glossy butterhead with deep-red outer leaves and a bright-green buttery heart. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing for a large head. Harvest whole when the head feels full and the center folds softly.

planting.note: A reliable green butterhead that performs well under cover and in the field.

directions: A smooth, bright-green butterhead forming a full head with a tender, buttery center. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest the whole head once it feels full and the interior leaves soften.

planting.note: An adaptable green butterhead resistant to bolting and downy mildew.

directions: A dependable green Boston-type butterhead that heads well across a range of seasons. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest the whole head once it fills out and the center leaves turn buttery.

planting.note: A large green Boston-type butterhead with broad disease resistance.

directions: A full-sized green butterhead forming heavy, tender heads with a sweet heart. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest whole when the head is full and the inner leaves feel soft.

planting.note: A green Boston-type butterhead that stands well in summer heat.

directions: A smooth green butterhead noted for heat tolerance and slow bolting. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing for a well-filled head. Harvest whole once the buttery center firms up.

planting.note: A classic light-green Boston butterhead best grown in cool weather.

directions: A traditional pale-green Boston butterhead with soft, loosely folded leaves and a mild heart. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing. Harvest whole once the head fills and the interior turns tender and buttery.

planting.note: A large old-fashioned Boston butterhead suited to cool spring and fall.

directions: A large heirloom butterhead dating to the 1800s, forming broad pale-green heads sometimes edged bronze. Sow shallowly and thin to wide spacing for full-sized heads. Harvest whole when the head feels heavy and the center is buttery.

planting.note: A French heirloom butterhead splashed red, also called Strawberry Cabbage.

directions: An heirloom French butterhead with green leaves boldly speckled and splashed deep red. Sow shallowly and thin to full spacing for best coloring. Harvest the whole head once the tender center folds and fills.

planting.note: A red little-gem type; close spacing keeps heads small and sweet.

directions: A compact little-gem romaine with red-blushed outer leaves and a crunchy green heart. Sow shallowly and thin to close spacing for tidy single-serving heads. Harvest whole when the small head firms up and colors well.

planting.note: Sow early spring for a summer crop or midsummer for a fall and winter harvest.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to stand about a foot apart. Its flat, oak-shaped blue-green leaves have purple stems and turn sweeter after a frost. Pick outer leaves once they reach hand size and let the center keep producing.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out hardened transplants a few weeks before the last frost.

directions: Transplant into rich, firm soil spaced roughly 15 inches apart. This F1 hybrid holds deep purple, tightly ruffled leaves that intensify in cold weather, making it both edible and ornamental. Harvest leaves from the bottom up and it will stand well into winter.

planting.note: Set out transplants in early spring or sow in midsummer for a hardy winter crop.

directions: Space plants about 15 inches apart in fertile ground and keep them evenly watered. This vigorous blue-green curled hybrid is extremely cold-tolerant and sweetens after hard frosts. Snap off the lower leaves as needed and the crown continues to push new growth.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool soil in spring or late summer for a cold-season harvest.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about a foot apart in loose, fertile soil. A cold-hardy sibling of Red Russian, it has frilly gray-green leaves on white ribs and exceptional winter durability. Harvest young for tender salad leaves or larger for cooking.

planting.note: One of the hardiest kales; sow in spring or late summer for fall through winter picking.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep and thin to give each plant a foot or more of room. This broad-leaved napus kale is prized for its extreme cold hardiness and mild, tender leaves. Cut outer foliage regularly to keep a steady supply through the cold months.

planting.note: A compact, cold-tolerant heirloom; sow spring or late summer.

directions: Sow directly and thin to about a foot apart in fertile ground. This dwarf, blue-green curled Scotch type stays low and holds its color and flavor through cold, resisting yellowing. Harvest tender leaves from the outside as the plant matures.

planting.note: Set out transplants in early spring or sow in summer for a fall crop.

directions: Transplant into firm, fertile soil about a foot apart. This uniform dwarf hybrid produces densely curled blue-green leaves on a tidy, high-yielding plant. Pick outer leaves continually and it will keep replacing them into cold weather.

planting.note: Color deepens with cold, so a fall crop shows the best purple.

directions: Set transplants about 15 inches apart in a sunny, fertile bed. The tightly curled leaves start green and turn a rich reddish-purple as temperatures drop, giving strong ornamental and culinary value. Harvest the lower leaves first and the plant stands well into winter.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; frilly leaves sweeten in cold weather.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to about a foot apart. A Red Russian selection with large frilly leaves and purple-red veining, it is tender, mild, and very cold hardy. Pick young for salads or full-size for braising, taking outer leaves as they mature.

planting.note: Savoy types are especially cold hardy and suit a fall crop.

directions: Set plants about 18 inches apart in fertile ground. This savoy type has deeply crinkled blue-green leaves and forms tender, mild heads that tolerate hard frost. Harvest firm heads and enjoy the crinkled leaves fresh or cooked.

planting.note: A quick mini-cabbage well suited to close spacing and small gardens.

directions: Set transplants as close as 10 inches apart to grow single-serving heads. This early hybrid makes uniform, softball-to-grapefruit sized green heads that hold well without cracking. Harvest when heads are firm; the tight size is ideal for one or two meals.

planting.note: A romanesco-type hybrid with spiraled lime-green heads.

directions: Space plants about 18 inches apart in fertile, evenly moist soil. This romanesco hybrid forms a striking chartreuse head made of pointed, spiraling fractal cones with a mild, nutty flavor. Harvest while the spirals are tight and firm and no blanching is required.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool soil in spring or late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to about 5 inches apart. This heirloom forms a swollen purple-skinned stem bulb just above the soil with crisp, mild white flesh. Harvest when the bulb is two to three inches across, before it turns woody.

planting.note: A fast cool-season heirloom for spring or fall sowing.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin seedlings to about 5 inches apart in fertile soil. This heirloom produces pale green, round stem bulbs with tender, sweet flesh. Pick while the bulb is small and crisp, since oversized bulbs become tough and fibrous.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant Southern heirloom for spring or fall, best after frost.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin plants to stand about two feet apart. This large, non-heading heirloom produces broad, tender blue-green leaves and tolerates both heat and frost. Pick the lower leaves as needed and the plant keeps producing from the crown.

planting.note: A compact, cold-hardy collard that sweetens with frost.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about 15 inches apart in fertile soil. This dwarf, slow-bolting heirloom holds compact, dark blue-green leaves and stands well through cold weather. Harvest the outer leaves and let the center continue growing for repeat picking.

planting.note: A loose-heading Southern heirloom, cold hardy and slow to bolt.

directions: Sow directly and thin to about two feet apart in rich soil. This heirloom forms a loose central head of thick, tender dark-green leaves and tolerates heat and cold. Harvest outer leaves through the season or cut the whole loose head once it firms.

planting.note: Best sown in mid to late summer for a fall crop to avoid bolting.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to about a foot apart, keeping soil consistently moist. This type forms a tall, narrow, upright head of crinkled pale-green leaves with a mild, sweet flavor. Harvest by cutting at the base once the head is tall and firm.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; harvest young as a loose-leaf green.

directions: Scatter or sow shallowly and thin to about 8 inches apart for full heads, or grow densely for cut greens. This loose, non-heading Chinese cabbage has frilly, light-green leaves on white stems and a tender, mild taste. Cut baby leaves early or harvest the whole rosette when full.

planting.note: A quick green-stemmed choi for spring or fall sowing.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about 8 inches apart, keeping the soil evenly moist. This green-stemmed baby choi forms spoon-shaped pale-green leaves on smooth jade stalks and matures quickly. Harvest whole while young and tender for the best flavor.

planting.note: Sow spring or fall; tolerates cold and grows fast for cut-and-come-again.

directions: Sow thinly a quarter inch deep and thin to a few inches apart for baby leaves or wider for full clumps. This Japanese mustard green forms a feathery rosette of deeply cut, mild-flavored leaves and regrows after cutting. Snip leaves young and it will resprout several times.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set out transplants a few weeks before the last spring frost.

directions: Set transplants about an inch deep with the neck at the soil line, spaced to give each mild bulb room to swell. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free, since these sweet, low-sulfur onions do not store long. Harvest once the tops soften and topple over, then cure in a dry, airy spot before use.

planting.note: A short-day type sown in fall in mild-winter regions for early summer bulbs.

directions: In warm-winter areas sow in autumn and transplant seedlings pencil-thick into fertile ground. As a short-day onion it begins bulbing early in the lengthening spring days, so keep it fed and watered through that stretch. Pull the large, mild, top-shaped bulbs when the necks weaken and use them soon, as they store poorly.

planting.note: The elongated red onion of Tropea, grown for its mild sweetness.

directions: Transplant seedlings shallowly into rich, well-worked soil in spring and keep them evenly watered. The long, spindle-shaped red bulbs are exceptionally mild and sweet, prized for grilling and salads. Pull them when the tops soften and use fresh, as this type does not keep long.

planting.note: An Italian torpedo-shaped onion often pulled young as a scallion or mature bulb.

directions: Sow indoors or direct and thin to spacing depending on whether you want scallions or full bottle-shaped bulbs. The elongated red-skinned onions are sweet and tender, excellent raw or roasted. Harvest young for green onions or later once the base swells and the tops relax.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant porcelain type ideal for fall planting in northern gardens.

directions: Set the large cloves two inches deep in autumn and cover with a thick straw mulch to protect against heaving. This porcelain hardneck yields a few big, easy-to-peel cloves per bulb and stands up to hard winters. Remove the scapes in early summer and dig the bulbs once several lower leaves have dried.

planting.note: Plant sets in early spring, or in fall where winters are mild, for clustered bulbs.

directions: Press each shallot bulb into loose soil with the tip just showing, spaced well apart to allow the cluster to multiply. Each planted bulb divides into a tight group of coppery-red shallots over the season. Harvest when the tops yellow and fall, then cure the bulbs thoroughly for long keeping.

planting.note: A French torpedo-shaped shallot often grown from seed for elongated bulbs.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings shallowly into rich, well-drained soil. This elegant banana shallot forms long, pink-brown bulbs with a delicate, refined flavor. Harvest once the tops soften and bend, then cure in a dry, airy place before storing.

planting.note: A seed-grown shallot valued for uniform bulbs and long storage.

directions: Sow indoors and set the young plants out an inch deep in fertile ground after hardening off. This hybrid produces uniform, red-brown shallots with a mild flavor and excellent keeping quality. Lift the bulbs when the foliage yellows and falls, then cure well before storing.

planting.note: A yellow-skinned multiplier shallot planted from sets in early spring.

directions: Push the sets into workable spring soil so the tips just peek out, leaving space for each to multiply. This variety forms large, golden-brown bulbs in a tidy cluster and resists bolting well. Harvest when the tops flop over and cure the bulbs for reliable long storage.

planting.note: A red-skinned multiplier shallot planted from sets in early spring.

directions: Set the bulbs shallowly into loose, well-drained soil in early spring, spacing them to allow clumping. Each divides into several firm, red-brown shallots with a strong, sweet shallot flavor. Harvest when the leaves yellow and topple, then cure fully for good winter storage.

planting.note: A hardy Scottish heirloom leek that stands through winter in the ground.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant pencil-thick seedlings into deep holes, dropping one plant per hole and watering it in. Draw soil up around the stems as they grow to blanch a long, tender white shaft. This tough old variety withstands frost, so harvest as needed straight through winter.

planting.note: A stout French heirloom leek grown for thick winter stems.

directions: Transplant seedlings into deep holes in fertile soil and water them in without backfilling, letting the stems thicken. This heirloom produces short, very fat shafts with blue-green leaves and stands well into cold weather. Blanch by mounding soil against the stems and harvest through fall and winter.

planting.note: A cold-hardy Dutch leek bred to hold quality through fall and winter.

directions: Start seed indoors and set transplants into deep dibble holes, watering rather than filling to leave room for the stem to expand. This reliable winter leek forms medium-thick, sturdy shafts with good frost tolerance. Hill up soil to blanch and harvest as needed from autumn onward.

planting.note: A heat-loving Southwestern multiplier onion that clumps and divides freely.

directions: Plant the small bulbs an inch deep in fall, spacing clumps to allow them to multiply into dense clusters. This heritage Sonoran multiplier thrives in heat and drier soils, dividing underground into many shallot-like bulbs. Snip the greens like scallions or lift and split the clumps, always replanting a few bulbs to keep the patch going.

planting.note: Sow once soil has warmed reliably past 60 F after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant kernels about one inch deep in short blocks of at least four rows so the tassels can pollinate the silks. Keep the bed evenly watered while the ears are filling and side-dress with nitrogen when plants are knee-high. Pick the plump white ears when the silks brown and a punctured kernel releases milky juice.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil warms to at least 60 F.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in blocks rather than single long rows to ensure well-filled ears of mixed yellow and white kernels. Feed heavily and water steadily through the growing season, especially during tasseling. Harvest as the silks dry and the yellow-and-white kernels reach full plumpness.

planting.note: This tender-kerneled type germinates best in soil that has warmed to 65 F or more.

directions: Wait for warm soil, then sow seed one inch deep in a compact block for good pollination. Provide consistent moisture and rich soil so the sweet yellow ears fill completely. Harvest when silks brown and kernels are milky, and refrigerate promptly to hold the tender-sweet flavor.

planting.note: An early white variety that can go in as soon as soil warms past 60 F.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in blocks of several rows for reliable pollination of this early white corn. Keep plants well fed and watered as ears develop. Because it matures quickly, watch the silks closely and pick at the milk stage for peak sweetness.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant open-pollinated variety suited to short-season northern gardens.

directions: Sow one inch deep in blocks; this open-pollinated corn tolerates cooler soil than most sweet types. Keep the patch weeded and evenly moist through ear fill. Harvest the yellow ears at the milk stage, and save seed from your best plants if you want to replant next year.

planting.note: An open-pollinated bicolor that germinates in cooler soils than hybrid sweet corn.

directions: Plant kernels an inch deep in a block, isolating from other corn if you plan to save seed. Give steady water and nitrogen through the season. Pick the bicolor ears at the milk stage; the flavor is best eaten fresh within a day of harvest.

planting.note: A very early dwarf ideal for short seasons and container or small-space growing.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep; the compact plants can be spaced a bit tighter than standard corn. Because stalks are short, plant in a dense block to help pollination. Harvest the small four-inch golden ears early, when kernels are at the tender milk stage.

planting.note: A late, large-eared yellow variety for main-season harvest.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block of at least four rows to fill the long ears. This tall, vigorous corn is a heavy feeder, so enrich the soil and water deeply through tasseling. Harvest the big yellow ears at the milk stage when the silks have dried.

planting.note: A sugary-enhanced white corn best sown into soil warmed to at least 60 F.

directions: Sow seed one inch deep in blocks so the tall stalks pollinate evenly. Provide rich soil, steady moisture, and a nitrogen boost as the plants grow. Pick the creamy white ears at the milk stage; the sugary-enhanced kernels hold their tenderness well after picking.

planting.note: A sugary-enhanced bicolor sown after the soil warms past 60 F.

directions: Plant kernels an inch deep in a compact block for full pollination of the bicolor ears. Keep the soil evenly moist and fertile throughout the season. Harvest when the silks brown and the yellow-and-white kernels reach the milk stage.

planting.note: A bicolor sugary-enhanced type for main-season harvest.

directions: Direct sow one inch deep in blocks of several rows to fill the large bicolor ears. Feed generously and water consistently, particularly as the ears form. Harvest at the milk stage when silks have dried and kernels are plump.

planting.note: This tender-seeded Japanese-bred type needs warm soil of 65 F or higher to germinate well.

directions: Wait for genuinely warm soil, then sow an inch deep in an isolated block to protect the sweetness from cross-pollination. Keep plants well watered and fed as ears develop. Harvest at the milk stage and eat soon after picking to enjoy the rich, high-sugar kernels.

planting.note: A bicolor supersweet-type best planted into warm soil past 65 F.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a warm, fertile block, isolating it from other corn types to keep the kernels sweet. Water deeply and steadily through ear fill. Pick the long bicolor ears at the milk stage; the kernels hold their sweetness for several days.

planting.note: A bicolor sugary-enhanced variety sown after soil warms past 60 F.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block of several short rows for good pollination. Keep the patch weed-free and well watered while the ears fill out. Harvest the tender bicolor ears at the milk stage when the silks turn brown.

planting.note: A classic mid-century yellow hybrid sown once soil reaches 60 F.

directions: Plant kernels an inch deep in a block for even pollination of this vigorous old-fashioned corn. Give it rich soil, ample nitrogen, and steady water through the season. Harvest the yellow ears at the milk stage; this normal-sugary type is best cooked soon after picking.

planting.note: An early bicolor good for a first sweet-corn harvest of the season.

directions: Sow one inch deep in a compact block for reliable pollination of this early bicolor. Keep the young plants weeded and evenly watered. Because it matures quickly, check the silks often and pick at the milk stage for the sweetest ears.

planting.note: This supersweet bicolor needs warm soil near 65 F for good germination.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep into warm soil and isolate this supersweet variety from other corn to preserve its sugar. Keep the block well watered so kernels fill fully. Harvest the crisp bicolor ears at the milk stage; the high-sugar kernels stay sweet for days.

planting.note: A long-season ornamental popcorn that needs the full warm growing season to dry down.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block for good pollination of the multicolored ears. Let the ears stay on the stalk until the husks are papery and the kernels are hard. Harvest, cure indoors for several weeks, then shell and store for popping or display.

planting.note: A blue-kerneled popcorn needing a long warm season to fully harden.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a compact block so the deep-blue ears pollinate well. Leave the ears on the plant until husks dry and kernels are rock-hard. Cure the shelled kernels a few weeks more before popping so their moisture level is right.

planting.note: A deep-red kerneled popcorn needing the full warm season to dry.

directions: Plant kernels an inch deep in a block of several rows for good ear fill. Let the striking red ears mature and dry fully on the stalk before harvest. Cure the shelled kernels indoors, then pop or use the colorful ears for display.

planting.note: A multicolored popcorn grown for both popping and autumn display.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a block to ensure the mixed-color kernels pollinate fully. Leave the ears to dry on the stalk until the husks are papery and kernels are hard. Harvest and cure, then enjoy the ears as ornaments or shell them for popping.

planting.note: A small-eared blue popcorn suited to compact plantings.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a tight block; the shorter plants pollinate best when grouped closely. Let the miniature blue ears dry fully on the stalk. Cure indoors after harvest, then shell for popping or keep the small ears for decoration.

planting.note: A rose-pink kerneled popcorn needing a long warm season.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block of several rows for good pollination. Allow the pink ears to remain on the stalk until husks and kernels are dry and hard. Harvest, cure a few weeks, then pop or display the distinctive rosy kernels.

planting.note: A tall, productive yellow popcorn needing a long warm season to dry down.

directions: Plant an inch deep in a block; these tall stalks benefit from close grouping for pollination. Let the yellow ears dry fully on the plant before harvest. Cure the shelled kernels several weeks so they pop crisply.

planting.note: A long-season red dent corn traditionally grown in the Southeast for meal and grits.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block of several rows for good wind pollination. Grow through the full warm season and let the deep-red ears dry hard on the stalk. Harvest, cure, and grind the kernels into rich cornmeal or grits.

planting.note: An Iowa heirloom dent corn suited to Midwestern growing conditions.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block for even pollination of the red-and-gold ears. Let the corn mature fully and dry on the stalk before harvest. Cure the ears indoors, then shell and grind for cornmeal or save as feed and seed.

planting.note: A Mexican heirloom with green kernels, traditionally used for green flour and tamales.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block to pollinate the emerald-green ears fully. Grow through the warm season and let the ears dry on the stalk until kernels harden. Harvest, cure, and grind the distinctive green kernels into flour.

planting.note: A Michigan heirloom yellow dent prized for cornmeal in shorter northern seasons.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block of several rows for reliable pollination. Let the golden ears mature and dry hard on the stalks. Cure the ears fully, then grind the kernels into a sweet-flavored yellow cornmeal.

planting.note: A Southern heirloom white dent on a red cob, needing a long warm season.

directions: Plant an inch deep in a block for good wind pollination of the white kernels. Grow through the full season and let the ears dry down on the tall stalks. Harvest and cure, then grind the white kernels into meal; the cobs turn a distinctive red.

planting.note: A Southern heirloom dent corn valued for high yields and long ears.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block of several rows so the tall stalks pollinate well. Grow through the warm season and allow the large ears to dry on the plant. Harvest, cure, and grind the kernels for cornmeal or use as livestock feed.

planting.note: An Illinois heirloom yellow dent long grown across the Corn Belt.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block for even pollination of this classic field corn. Grow through the season and let the golden ears dry hard on the stalks. Cure the ears indoors, then shell for meal, feed, or seed.

planting.note: A Pennsylvania heirloom dent corn known for dependable yields.

directions: Plant kernels an inch deep in a block of several rows for good wind pollination. Grow through the warm season and let the ears dry down fully on the stalk. Harvest, cure, and grind the yellow kernels into meal or keep as seed.

planting.note: A Pacific Northwest-bred flint corn adapted to cooler, shorter seasons.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block for good pollination of the ruby, gold, and orange ears. Let the ears dry hard on the stalk before harvest. Cure the shelled kernels, then grind into flavorful polenta or cornmeal.

planting.note: An extremely early, short-statured flint from the Gaspe Peninsula, ideal for very short seasons.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a dense block, since the tiny plants pollinate best when grouped closely. Let the small ears dry on the stalk until kernels are hard. Harvest, cure, and grind the flinty kernels, or save the fast-maturing variety for cold-climate seed.

common_name: Rhode Island White Cap Flint Corn

planting.note: A New England heirloom flint traditionally used for johnnycakes.

directions: Plant an inch deep in a block of several rows for good wind pollination of the white ears. Let the ears dry down on the stalk until the kernels are hard and flinty. Cure and grind into fine white meal, prized for traditional johnnycakes.

planting.note: A New England yellow flint adapted to cool northern gardens.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block for reliable pollination of the golden ears. Let the corn dry hard on the stalk before harvest. Cure the shelled kernels, then grind into a rich yellow cornmeal or polenta.

planting.note: A Cherokee heirloom flour corn with striking blue-and-white eagle markings on white ears.

directions: Direct sow an inch deep in a block for good pollination of the mostly white ears. Grow through the warm season and let the ears dry hard on the stalk. Harvest, cure, and grind the soft kernels into fine white flour.

planting.note: A modern open-pollinated flour corn bred for early, easily-ground grain in cool climates.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in a block for good pollination of the multicolored ears. Let the ears dry on the stalk until the soft kernels are fully hardened. Cure and grind into flour; the diverse colors can be sorted for different-colored meals.

planting.note: Sow only after nights are warm, since this African green thrives in true summer heat.

directions: Scatter the tiny seeds shallowly in hot soil and thin seedlings once they have a few true leaves. As the upright stalks lengthen, pick the tender young leaves and tips, which develop a prized silky texture when stewed. Frequent harvesting encourages side branching and delays the seed pods, which are best avoided in the kitchen.

planting.note: Surface sow in warm weather, as light and heat trigger germination of this succulent.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil surface without burying it, since it needs light to sprout in warm ground. The low, fleshy rosettes tolerate drought and heat and grow with little fuss once established. Snip the crunchy, lemon-tart stem tips as they lengthen, and the plants rebranch for several cuttings before flowering.

planting.note: Sow in spring for summer greens or late summer for an overwintering crop.

directions: Sow seed clusters an inch deep and thin the resulting clumps to single plants. This thin-stemmed leaf beet resists bolting far better than true spinach and cropping continues for many months. Harvest the outer leaves regularly from the center outward, and a spring sowing will often keep producing well into the following year.

planting.note: Sow into wet, salty soil in spring for this coastal annual succulent.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly in constantly moist, sandy ground, and add sea salt or seawater to mimic its saline home. The jointed, leafless stems stay crisp and briny when grown wet, so never let the bed dry out. Snip the tender upper branches with scissors while young, before the stems turn woody and fibrous late in summer.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; this bladder campion returns reliably each year.

directions: Sow shallowly in well-drained soil and thin to give the clumps room to bush out. Pick the young, sweet-grassy shoot tips in spring before the puffy white flowers open, when they are prized for risottos and omelets. Regular cutting keeps a supply of tender growth, and the drought-tolerant crowns come back stronger each season.

planting.note: Sow after the soil has warmed, since amaranth germinates poorly when cold.

directions: Sow the tiny seed a quarter inch deep in warm soil and thin seedlings to stand a hand-width apart. This heat-loving leaf type produces soft green-and-red foliage that cooks up like a rich spinach. Begin cutting outer leaves and pinching tops at about a foot tall, which keeps the plant branching and delays bolting.

planting.note: Direct sow into hot soil, as this tropical amaranth demands warmth.

directions: Broadcast or row-sow the fine seed shallowly and thin to prevent crowding. A staple leafy green across the tropics, it grows fast and yields tender leaves that cook down quickly and richly. Harvest young whole plants for a tender crop, or pick leaves and tips repeatedly from taller stands before they flower.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once the weather is warm and settled.

directions: Root stem cuttings in damp soil, since this striking green with purple leaf undersides is grown vegetatively. Give it rich, moist soil and light shade in the hottest climates, and overwinter it indoors where frost occurs. Harvest the tender tips and young leaves often for stir-fries and salads, cutting back leggy stems to keep it compact.

planting.note: Start under cover in spring, then plant into damp, salt-tolerant ground.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow on before setting plants into constantly moist soil that can be watered with a little salt. The fleshy, slightly salty leaves are a coastal delicacy picked young before the mauve daisy flowers appear. Harvest the tender rosette leaves and shoot tips through spring and summer, cropping lightly so the perennial clumps persist.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil, as this fast tropical green thrives in heat and humidity.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly in warm, rich soil and thin the seedlings to give them room. A popular West African and Caribbean green, it grows quickly and yields tender, succulent leaves and stems that cook down softly. Harvest the top growth repeatedly, which keeps the plant lush, and pinch out flower stems to favor leaf production.

planting.note: Transplant after frost, treating it much like its relative the tomato.

directions: Start the small seed indoors and set out sturdy transplants once the soil is warm. A widely grown leafy green across East and West Africa, it forms bushy plants whose leaves are picked young for cooking. Harvest the tender leaves and shoot tips regularly, and always cook them well, since the raw foliage and green berries should not be eaten.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil, as germination is slow and uneven when cold.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm soil and be patient, since sprouting can be irregular. A treasured traditional green in eastern and southern Africa, it grows fast in the heat and yields pungent leaves picked while young. Harvest leaves and tender tips continually and pinch the spidery flower stalks to keep the plant producing tender foliage rather than seed.

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in warm soil, as this shrub is grown vegetatively.

directions: Root stout stem cuttings in moist soil, since this West African shrub is propagated from stems rather than seed. Grow it in rich, well-drained ground and cut it back to keep a supply of fresh leaves within reach. Pick mature leaves and, as is traditional, wash and squeeze or soak them before cooking to wash out much of the strong bitterness.

planting.note: Sow only after the soil is thoroughly warm and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow about one inch deep once soil holds steady warmth. This Texas heirloom bears red-tinged pods on tall drought-tolerant plants that shrug off summer heat. Cut pods every day or two while they are two to four inches long and still snap cleanly.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil a week or more after the last frost.

directions: Plant seed an inch deep in a bed that receives full sun all day. This early productive heirloom sets long slender green pods that stay tender if picked young. Harvest daily and keep pods cut to push the plant into continuous production.

planting.note: Wait for warm settled weather before sowing this heat-loving crop.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and thin seedlings to a foot apart. Plants grow tall with smooth spineless green pods prized for gumbo. Pick pods at three to four inches, cutting them before they turn woody.

planting.note: This long-season type needs warm soil and a full summer to crop well.

directions: Plant soaked seed an inch deep in the hottest spot you have. Tall plants bear unusual creamy-white to pale-green pods that stay tender at a good size. Harvest often while the pale pods snap easily to keep new ones forming.

planting.note: Sow directly once nights stay warm and the soil has heated up.

directions: Set seed an inch deep in full sun and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouts appear. This Southern heirloom carries plump red-blushed pods on red-tinted stems and makes a handsome as well as productive plant. Cut pods young, since the red fades to green during cooking.

planting.note: Give this vigorous type warm soil and room to reach full height.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep and space plants generously because they grow very tall. The long curved pods stay surprisingly tender even at seven or eight inches. Pick regularly, and let a few pods dry on the plant if you want to save seed.

planting.note: Direct sow when the soil is warm and settled after the last frost.

directions: Plant soaked seed an inch deep in full sun with wide spacing. This spineless heirloom is famed for ribless pods that stay tender to remarkable lengths. Harvest every day during peak season to keep the towering plants cranking out pods.

planting.note: Sow after frost once the ground has warmed through.

directions: Set soaked seed an inch deep in full sun and thin to a foot apart. This Virginia family heirloom produces slender red-ribbed pods on red-stemmed plants over a long season. Pick pods young and often for the most tender eating.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil once frost danger is well past.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep and keep moist until germination. This old variety bears smooth round pale pods without ridges, and they stay tender a bit longer than ridged types. Harvest at three to five inches, cutting the stems cleanly with shears.

planting.note: A heat lover that wants warm soil and full southern sun.

directions: Plant soaked seed an inch deep in a hot, sunny bed. This Texas border heirloom is notably drought tolerant and keeps producing through blistering summers. Pick the smooth green pods young and often to maintain quality.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost is past and the soil is warm.

directions: Set seed an inch deep in full sun and thin to a foot apart. This South Carolina Lowcountry heirloom yields tender green pods valued in coastal cooking. Cut pods every day while they are short and snap easily.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil in late spring.

directions: Sow soaked seed an inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Plants produce spineless green pods over a long and reliable season. Harvest often at three to four inches to keep the pods tender.

planting.note: Give this tall cowhorn type warm soil and plenty of room.

directions: Plant soaked seed an inch deep and space plants well apart. The long curved pods remain tender to a larger size than most okra. Pick regularly and stake in windy sites since plants grow very tall.

planting.note: Direct sow once the soil warms; the compact habit suits shorter seasons.

directions: Sow seed an inch deep in full sun and thin to a foot apart. This shorter early heirloom sets long ribbed green pods low on compact plants, making harvest easy. Pick pods young and often to keep the plants cropping.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm soil after all frost danger passes.

directions: Plant soaked seed an inch deep in a hot sunny bed with wide spacing. This Georgia family heirloom grows tall and stout with large spineless pods that stay tender. Harvest daily during summer to keep pods from toughening.

planting.note: Transplant after the soil is warm and settled in late spring.

directions: Sow indoors in warmth and transplant deeply into rich, sunny ground. This selection produces bright red calyces heavily once short autumn days trigger flowering. Harvest the tender calyces regularly for tea and preserves before frost cuts the plants down.

planting.note: Set transplants out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out after frost into full sun. These compact sprawling plants ripen abundant small husked fruit with a sweet pineapple-citrus taste. Collect the fruit after it falls, and store the intact husks for a few weeks at room temperature.

planting.note: Transplant after frost and give the vines a tall support.

directions: Start seed indoors and plant vigorous vines out after frost at the base of a sturdy trellis. This Andean climber bears pale green pods that are eaten young raw or cooked like a mild pepper when hollowed. Pick the pods small and tender, since older ones fill with seed and toughen.

planting.note: Set out after frost onto a strong tall trellis.

directions: Start indoors and transplant robust vines out after frost against a heavy support. This large-fruited strain produces plump green pods up to six inches that are stuffed and cooked like peppers. Harvest young for raw eating or a bit larger to hollow and fill.

planting.note: Transplant after frost and provide a trellis for the fast vines.

directions: Start seed indoors and set plants out after frost near a trellis they can climb. This smaller spiny-fruited achocha crops heavily with little fuss and self-seeds easily. Pick the plump little pods young to eat raw in salads or lightly cooked.

planting.note: Plant small side corms once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Set the small clustered corms 3 to 4 inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil, keeping them evenly wet all season. Unlike dasheen, eddoe tolerates slightly drier ground once established but still resents drought. Harvest the clustered daughter corms after the foliage collapses, saving a few for replanting.

planting.note: Plant corms after the soil warms; this cultivar needs a long, hot season.

directions: This large-cormed cultivar, prized for taro chips, wants deep, rich, permanently moist soil and full sun. Bury the seed corms about 5 inches deep and keep water flowing all summer. Lift the big central corms after the leaves yellow, then cure and cook thoroughly before eating.

planting.note: Plant tuber setts after all frost danger passes and soil is warm.

directions: Plant tuber pieces or the central corm head about 4 inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil that stays moist. Tannia tolerates somewhat drier and better-drained ground than true taro and forms a cluster of side tubers around a central corm. Dig the whole cluster after the leaves decline, harvesting the finger-like side cormels for the kitchen.

planting.note: Plant corms as the warm rainy season begins for best bulking.

directions: Set the large corms about 6 inches deep in loose, deeply worked, well-drained but moisture-holding soil enriched with compost. A single mottled stalk unfurls into an umbrella of leaflets while the corm swells underground over the season. Harvest the massive corm after the leaf dies back, and cook it thoroughly to neutralize acridity.

planting.note: Plant tuber setts once soil warms; the vine needs a long hot season on a strong trellis.

directions: Plant tuber pieces or minisetts a few inches deep in deep, loose, well-drained mounds and give the twining stems a tall, sturdy support to climb. The winged stems can climb many feet while a single large tuber forms below over most of the year. Harvest once the vine yellows and dies back, digging carefully to lift the long tuber intact.

planting.note: Plant small tubers once soil is warm and settled.

directions: Plant the small oval tubers a few inches deep in loose, fertile mounds and give the somewhat spiny vine light support. This species forms a cluster of many small, sweet, easily cooked tubers near the surface rather than one giant root. Harvest the clustered tubers after the foliage yellows, lifting them gently since they bruise readily.

planting.note: Plant tuber pieces after frost when the ground is warm.

directions: Plant tuber setts in loose, rich, well-drained soil and let the winged stems climb a low trellis. Native to tropical America, this yam produces a cluster of small, fine-textured tubers with white, yellow, or purple flesh. Harvest the tuber cluster when the vine dies back, and replant a few of the best for the next crop.

planting.note: Plant aerial bulbils after frost; grow only where its spread can be controlled.

directions: Plant a bulbil at the base of a strong trellis in warm, fertile soil and let the twining vine climb. This species is grown for the round tubers that form in the leaf axils above ground rather than below. Gather the aerial bulbils as they mature, but note only certain edible strains are safe and the plant is invasive in warm regions, so contain it carefully.

planting.note: Inoculate poplar or willow logs in spring, or run supplemented sawdust blocks indoors year-round.

directions: Colonize a supplemented sawdust block or plug soft hardwood logs such as poplar and willow, holding warm room temperatures during the spawn run. When clusters of slender brown-capped mushrooms push up on their pale stems, keep humidity high and give steady fresh air. Harvest the tight bundles before the caps flatten fully, cutting them at the base as a group.

planting.note: Lay spawn into a shaded outdoor wood-chip bed once the soil has warmed in spring.

directions: Build a shaded bed of fresh hardwood chips or straw, layering in sawdust spawn as you go, then top with a thin cover of chips and water well. Keep the bed evenly moist through summer; wine-red caps with white stems appear a few months after the mycelium runs. Cut mushrooms while the caps are still domed and the gills pale, and refresh the bed with new chips each season.

planting.note: Grown indoors on composted substrate year-round wherever a cool, dark room can be kept humid.

directions: Spawn a bed of pasteurized straw-manure compost and hold it warm and dark until the surface is knit with white mycelium. Add a moist casing layer of peat and lime, then drop the temperature and raise humidity to trigger pinning. Harvest small brown buttons as cremini, or leave them to open into broad flat portobello caps before twisting them free.

planting.note: A laboratory-style grow on sterilized grain-and-broth jars kept indoors under controlled light and temperature.

directions: Inoculate sterilized jars of enriched grain or rice broth with liquid culture and incubate them in the dark until the surface turns orange. Move the colonized jars into indirect light with cool temperatures to trigger the upright, club-shaped orange fruit bodies. Harvest the bright stromata once they reach full length and firm texture, cutting them cleanly at the substrate.

planting.note: Inoculate hardwood logs or sawdust bags in spring and fruit through warm, humid weather.

directions: Plug fresh hardwood logs or fill bags with supplemented sawdust and let the mycelium run under warm, damp, shaded conditions. Rubbery, ear-shaped brown fruit bodies form on the wood and expand quickly when kept wet. Pick them while still gelatinous and flexible, then dry the surplus for storage.

planting.note: Grows readily on straw or supplemented sawdust indoors, or on hardwood logs outdoors in the warm season.

directions: Colonize pasteurized straw or a supplemented sawdust block, holding warm room temperatures until the substrate is fully white. Unlike true oysters, single thick-stemmed caps form rather than shelved clusters, so give steady fresh air and high humidity as they develop. Harvest each firm cap while the margin is still slightly inrolled.

planting.note: Grown on supplemented sawdust or wood in warm, very humid conditions, often indoors.

directions: Inoculate supplemented hardwood sawdust with the jelly fungus together with its companion mold, which the fungus needs to fruit well. Keep the block warm and near saturation humidity while the translucent white, ruffled lobes swell out of the substrate. Harvest the whole gelatinous rosette once it reaches full size, then dry it for storage.

planting.note: Plug freshly cut hardwood logs or stumps and wait one to two years for the first flush.

directions: Hammer spawn plugs into a fresh oak or other hardwood log or stump and seal each hole with wax. The fungus colonizes slowly, so expect the first bright sulphur-yellow and orange shelves only after a year or more. Harvest the young, tender outer edges of the brackets while they are still soft and moist, leaving woody older growth behind.

planting.note: Inoculate conifer logs, stumps, or buried billets and allow a long colonization period.

directions: Plug or sawdust-inoculate conifer logs, stumps, or partially buried billets, since this species prefers softwood such as pine. Colonization is slow, and the ruffled, cream-colored rosette resembling a sea sponge typically appears near the wood base in autumn. Cut the whole frilly head while it is pale and firm, before the edges begin to brown.

planting.note: Spawn a rich outdoor bed of compost and straw in the cool, damp part of the season.

directions: Mix spawn into a bed of compost, straw, and soil in a shaded, well-watered spot during cool weather. Tall, shaggy white cylinders push up quickly and must be gathered promptly. Harvest while the caps are still closed and white, because within a day they dissolve from the bottom edge into a black inky liquid.

planting.note: Layer spawn into a composting bed of leaves and straw for a late-season flush.

directions: Build a shaded bed of composting leaf litter and straw and mix spawn through it, keeping it moist as the mycelium establishes. The lilac-tinged caps typically fruit in autumn and can continue into mild winter weather. Harvest the firm, violet-hued mushrooms while young, and always cook them thoroughly before eating.

planting.note: A tropical mushroom grown on pasteurized straw with a casing layer in warm, humid conditions.

directions: Spawn pasteurized straw in bags and incubate them in the warmth until fully colonized, then apply a moist casing of soil and coir. This heat-tolerant species fruits well through hot weather, producing sturdy, pure-white caps on thick stems. Harvest the firm milky-white mushrooms once the caps open, and note they hold their quality and shelf life unusually well.

planting.note: Inoculate living or freshly cut birch, accepting that development takes several years.

directions: Inoculate birch logs or living birch trees with spawn, since this cold-climate fungus grows almost exclusively on birch. The hard, charcoal-black conk called a sclerotium builds very slowly and may take several years to reach a harvestable size. Cut mature conks from the wood, leaving some behind so the fungus can continue producing.

planting.note: Plant inoculated oak or hazel seedlings into limed, well-drained ground; harvest comes only after years.

directions: Plant nursery-raised oak or hazel seedlings whose roots have been inoculated with truffle spores into alkaline, sharply drained soil, liming the ground first if needed. The fungus lives in partnership with the tree roots and produces its underground fruit bodies only after four or more years of establishment. Harvest the ripe black truffles by digging where a trained dog or pig scents them beneath the soil in winter.

planting.note: Grown indoors on trays year-round rather than tied to an outdoor season.

directions: Broadcast the large seed thickly across a moist tray of shallow mix and press in gently without covering deeply. Keep under a weighted lid for two days to build strong stems, then uncover and light brightly. Snip the spicy stems just above the growing medium once the seed leaves fully open and the first true leaf hints.

planting.note: Sown on trays indoors in any season for a quick pink-stemmed crop.

directions: Scatter seed densely over damp shallow soil and firm it in for even sprouting. Blackout the tray for a couple of days to draw up rosy stems, then move to bright light to green the leaves. Cut when the cotyledons are fully expanded, prizing the pink stems and pungent bite.

planting.note: A purple-stemmed radish grown indoors on trays for color and heat.

directions: Sow thickly onto moist medium and press flat so every seed contacts soil. Cover to exclude light for two to three days, encouraging tall violet stems, then expose to steady light. Harvest with scissors at the base once the seed leaves stand upright and colored.

planting.note: A staple microgreen grown indoors on trays throughout the year.

directions: Spread the small round seed evenly and densely over a shallow tray of moist mix, misting to settle it. Keep dark and covered for two to three days, then bring into bright light and mist twice daily. Cut the mild, tender stems just above the soil when the twin leaves are green and open.

planting.note: Sown on indoor trays for a sharp, slightly bitter cutting green.

directions: Broadcast seed thickly on damp shallow soil and firm gently. Hold under cover in the dark for a few days before exposing to bright light. Snip at the base when cotyledons are fully open, expecting a peppery, mustard-like flavor.

planting.note: A purple-leaved mustard grown on trays for a hot, wasabi-like microgreen.

directions: Sow the fine seed densely over moist mix and mist rather than bury it. Give a short blackout period, then plenty of bright light to deepen the burgundy color. Cut when the small leaves show purple veining, delivering a strong horseradish heat.

planting.note: A vigorous green mustard grown indoors for pungent cutting greens.

directions: Scatter seed evenly and thickly on damp shallow soil, misting to keep the surface moist. Cover for two days, then uncover into bright light. Harvest with scissors once the bright green seed leaves stand up, expecting a peppery bite.

planting.note: A mild Japanese mustard green grown on indoor trays year-round.

directions: Broadcast seed densely over moist mix and firm gently for even germination. Keep covered and dark briefly, then bring into bright light. Cut the feathery, mildly peppery greens at the base once cotyledons open fully.

planting.note: A fast peppery cress grown on trays or damp fiber indoors.

directions: Sprinkle the mucilaginous seed thickly over a moist surface and do not cover, since it needs light to sprout. Keep the medium constantly damp as the seed forms a gel and roots quickly. Cut the tangy, peppery seedlings within a week or so, once the tiny leaves unfurl.

planting.note: A tiny aquatic-loving cress grown on constantly moist trays indoors.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on a wet tray and leave it uncovered, keeping the medium saturated at all times. Mist frequently or bottom-water so the seedlings never dry out. Harvest the small, peppery greens when they reach a couple of inches, cutting just above the medium.

planting.note: A deep-red-stemmed beet grown on trays for striking microgreens.

directions: Soak the corky seed clusters overnight, then sow densely and cover lightly since beets germinate best with some darkness. Keep evenly moist and expect uneven, staggered sprouting over a week. Grow under bright light to intensify the crimson stems and harvest when the first leaves show dark veining.

planting.note: A classic red beet grown on indoor trays for earthy, colorful shoots.

directions: Pre-soak the seed balls to speed sprouting, then broadcast thickly and cover with a thin layer of mix. Keep moist and be patient as germination spreads over several days. Cut the red-stemmed, green-leaved shoots when the seed leaves are fully expanded.
